items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: A strong personal study opens with a:
items.why: Correct answer: Thesis question. “Biography” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Formal qualities of the work. “Biography of the artist” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A personal study word limit is 3000 words. If 60% is analysis, how many words are devoted to analysis?
items.why: Exact answer: 1800. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
